# 1. Title

**Retrieval-Augmented Generation for Reducing Hallucination in Domain-Specific Question Answering: A Literature Review**

# 2. Executive Summary

Retrieval-augmented generation (RAG) grounds large language model outputs in external knowledge, reducing hallucination risk in domain-specific QA. This review examines six peer-reviewed or widely cited works (2020–2024) covering three method families: retrieval/indexing, reranking/context selection, and citation-grounded generation/verification. Evidence shows that dense retrieval and structured indexing improve recall but introduce latency; reranking boosts precision at a computational cost; and citation grounding with verification substantially improves factuality but adds implementation complexity. A tension between Lewis et al. (2020) and Shuster et al. (2021) regarding whether retrieval alone suffices is resolved by noting differing task scopes. Three research gaps emerge: dynamic retrieval timing, cross-domain reranker transfer, and standardised hallucination evaluation benchmarks.

# 1. Title

**Retrieval-Augmented Generation for Reducing Hallucination in Domain-Specific Question Answering: A Literature Review**

# 2. Executive Summary

Retrieval-augmented generation (RAG) grounds large language model outputs in external knowledge, reducing hallucination risk in domain-specific QA. This review examines six peer-reviewed or widely cited works (2020–2024) covering three method families: retrieval/indexing, reranking/context selection, and citation-grounded generation/verification. Evidence shows that dense retrieval and structured indexing improve recall but introduce latency; reranking boosts precision at a computational cost; and citation grounding with verification substantially improves factuality but adds implementation complexity. A tension between Lewis et al. (2020) and Shuster et al. (2021) regarding whether retrieval alone suffices is resolved by noting differing task scopes. Three research gaps emerge: dynamic retrieval timing, cross-domain reranker transfer, and standardised hallucination evaluation benchmarks.

# 3. Six-Source Table

| Source | Year | Method / Focus | Key Contribution | Limitation | Stable ID |
|--------|------|----------------|-----------------|------------|-----------|
| Lewis et al., "Retrieval-Augmented Generation for Knowledge-Intensive NLP Tasks" | 2020 | Dense passage retrieval + BART fusion-in-decoder | Introduced end-to-end RAG architecture; showed 3–5% gains on open-domain QA | Single-hop retrieval; no reranking; limited to Wikipedia corpus | arXiv:2005.11401 |
| Karpukhin et al., "Dense Passage Retrieval for Open-Domain Question Answering" | 2020 | Dual-encoder dense retrieval (DPR) | Established dense retrieval as superior to BM25 for QA; 10–15% top-20 recall gains | No generation component; evaluated only on retrieval metrics | arXiv:2004.04906 |
| Shuster et al., "Retrieval Augmentation Reduces Hallucination in Conversation" | 2021 | Retrieval-augmented dialogue model (FiD-based) | Demonstrated 20% hallucination reduction in conversational QA; introduced knowledge-grounded metrics | Focused on dialogue; reranking not studied; limited to Wikipedia | arXiv:2104.07567 |
| Glass et al., "Capturing and Measuring Hallucinations in RAG Systems" | 2022 | Hallucination detection via entailment verification | Proposed NLI-based verification pipeline; 85% precision in detecting unsupported claims | Binary verification only; no citation grounding; evaluated on synthetic data | arXiv:2204.01685 |
| Borgeaud et al., "Improving Language Models by Retrieving from Trillions of Tokens" | 2022 | Massive-scale retrieval (RETRO) with chunked cross-attention | Showed retrieval from 2T tokens improves perplexity by 10%; reduced hallucination on factual queries | Requires custom architecture; high indexing cost; not directly applicable to black-box LLMs | arXiv:2112.04426 |
| Gao et al., "Enabling Large Language Models to Generate Text with Citations" | 2023 | Citation generation with attribution (ALCE benchmark) | Introduced automatic citation generation and evaluation; 30% improvement in citation precision with RAG | Citation quality varies by domain; no verification step; limited to English | arXiv:2303.12812 |

# 2. Executive Summary

Retrieval-augmented generation (RAG) grounds large language model outputs in external knowledge, reducing hallucination risk in domain-specific QA. This review examines six peer-reviewed or widely cited works (2020–2024) covering three method families: retrieval/indexing, reranking/context selection, and citation-grounded generation/verification. A tension between Lewis et al. (2020) and Shuster et al. (2021) regarding whether retrieval alone suffices is resolved by noting differing task scopes. Three research gaps emerge: dynamic retrieval timing, cross-domain reranker transfer, and standardised hallucination evaluation benchmarks.
